Skip to main content
Enterprise Systems Design  ·  Operational Workflow Redesign

When the tools fail,
the operation fails.

A research-driven systems design exploration into how fragmented logistics dispatch operations could be unified into a single, role-aware platform — reducing cognitive overhead and structural compliance risk.

RoleLead Product Designer
TypeSelf-initiated concept
Timeline6 months
PlatformWeb — Desktop-first
ⓘ

Research-driven concept project. This is a self-initiated systems design exploration grounded in secondary research, industry pattern analysis, and simulated workflow modelling. It is not a commissioned or deployed product. All personas, workflows, and interaction patterns are derived from published industry research on mid-to-large logistics operations.

01  /  The Problem

$9 trillion moves annually. Much of it still dispatched via Excel.

Secondary research into mid-to-large logistics operations reveals a structural pattern: despite the scale of the industry, dispatch operations are routinely managed across three to five disconnected systems — a TMS or spreadsheet for deal tracking, a separate GPS portal for fleet visibility, document templates for compliance, and informal messaging tools for real-time coordination.

The fragmentation isn’t incidental. It’s the accumulated result of point-solution procurement over time. Each tool solves one problem and creates three others. Industry analysis consistently surfaces the same failure modes: context-switching overhead, compliance steps skipped under pressure, and management visibility that depends entirely on dispatchers choosing to surface information.

The design question: What would it look like to redesign the dispatch operation as a unified system — one that treats compliance, fleet visibility, and deal tracking as interconnected concerns, not separate tools?

3–5 Disconnected tools typical in mid-to-large logistics dispatch operations
40min Estimated per-shift context-switching overhead based on workflow simulation
0 Proactive delay alerts in typical legacy setups — managers rely on manual escalation
02  /  Role & Scope

End-to-end system design. Not a UI exercise.

This exploration covers the full design process: problem framing, operational research synthesis, information architecture, interaction design, accessibility evaluation, and iterative critique. The goal was to practise systems-level thinking on a domain with genuine structural complexity — not to produce a polished interface for its own sake.

01

Research synthesis

Secondary research, industry reports, and workflow simulation to model realistic dispatch operations and failure modes.

02

Systems & IA design

Role-aware information architecture, module hierarchy, and data flow design across two distinct operational personas.

03

Interaction design

Core UX decisions, component patterns, and progressive disclosure mechanisms for high-density operational interfaces.

04

Accessibility & evaluation

WCAG 2.2 AA compliance throughout, formal heuristic evaluation, and simulated cognitive walkthrough against four operational tasks.

03  /  Research Insights

Three patterns that shaped every decision.

Research synthesis drew on published workflow analyses of logistics dispatch environments, industry reports on compliance failure modes, and operational pattern documentation. Simulated workflow modelling was used to map realistic task sequences and identify friction points.

⇄

Pattern 1 — The context-switching tax

Industry workflow analyses consistently show dispatchers toggling between three or more systems within a single task sequence. Each context switch carries a cognitive cost and introduces an opportunity for data transcription error. Workflow simulation suggests this overhead could account for 30–45 minutes of avoidable friction per shift.

⚠

Pattern 2 — Compliance as an afterthought

In fragmented setups, compliance verification sits outside the primary workflow — typically a separate SharePoint lookup or manual document check. Industry research on logistics compliance failures indicates that steps with no systemic enforcement are routinely skipped under time pressure. The risk compounds: deploying a carrier with lapsed insurance can have serious legal and financial consequences.

◎

Pattern 3 — Reactive management visibility

Operations managers in fragmented environments typically have no live view of fleet state. Visibility depends on dispatchers actively surfacing information — a fundamentally unreliable mechanism. The result is that strategic oversight collapses into reactive firefighting, with managers learning about problems only after they’ve escalated.

Design mandate: Build a unified, role-aware system that embeds compliance into the primary workflow, eliminates context-switching through data integration, and gives operations managers live visibility without adding cognitive burden to dispatchers.

04  /  Operational Personas

Two roles. Fundamentally different information needs.

The system serves two distinct operational profiles. Their needs are not just different in degree — they require different information hierarchies, different interaction patterns, and different alert semantics. Designing for one without degrading the other was a core structural challenge.

AL

Alex — Dispatch Coordinator

“I need to know instantly if a truck is delayed so I can reroute.”

Needs: dense tabular data, rapid filtering, high-contrast alert states, and uninterrupted workflow continuity. Any full-page navigation mid-task is a failure mode.

SA

Sarah — Operations Manager

“I need a system that prevents deploying unlicensed carriers.”

Needs: live KPI aggregation, compliance risk visibility, and drill-down capability. Requires strategic overview, not row-level operational density.

05  /  Simulated Workflow

Five steps. Five structural failure points.

Workflow simulation of the as-is dispatch lifecycle — modelled against industry-standard logistics operation patterns — surfaces a consistent finding: each step in the process either duplicates data from a previous system, introduces compliance risk through manual verification, or requires a full context switch to a separate tool.

1

Deal logged via email → manual spreadsheet entry

Contract arrives by email. Dispatcher re-enters all cargo data into a spreadsheet, duplicating information already captured in the sales system.

↯ Structural: every manual transcription step introduces error risk
2

Compliance check → disconnected document store

Dispatcher navigates to a separate SharePoint or file system to verify carrier insurance validity. No systemic enforcement means this step is skippable under pressure.

↯ Structural: compliance verification outside the primary flow will be skipped
3

Fleet assignment → third-party GPS portal

Dispatcher opens a separate web portal to locate the nearest idle vehicle. No cargo-type validation exists between the deal system and the fleet system.

↯ Structural: cross-system vehicle assignment enables cargo/vehicle mismatches
4

CMR document → Word template → PDF → email

Data already in the system is manually retyped into a Word template, exported as PDF, and emailed to the driver. The data duplication here is total.

↯ Structural: rekeying existing data is pure overhead with no information gain
5

Shipment monitoring → phone calls and manual portal checks

No systemic delay alerting. Visibility depends on drivers calling in or dispatchers actively checking a separate GPS portal.

↯ Structural: reactive-only visibility means problems surface after they’ve cascaded
06  /  System Constraints & Assumptions

What the system depends on. What it assumes.

Designing a unified platform requires making the technical dependencies explicit. These constraints shaped architectural decisions throughout — particularly around data latency, role separation, and failure state handling.

Constraint
Design implication
GPS / telematics API
Fleet geolocation depends on a real-time third-party data feed. The UI must handle feed latency and outage states explicitly — a silently blank map is a critical failure. Explicit “GPS disconnected” empty states were a V2 requirement.
Document generation
CMR / Bill of Lading generation assumes an integration between the deals data model and a document rendering service. The design assumes this happens asynchronously — loading states and toast confirmations are required to prevent duplicate submissions.
Multi-user concurrency
Multiple dispatchers may assign fleet to the same deal simultaneously. The design assumes optimistic locking at the data layer, with conflict resolution surfacing in the UI as an inline warning rather than a blocking error.
Role-based permissions
Dispatchers and operations managers see different data densities and action permissions. The IA and component hierarchy are designed to support role-based rendering — the same view serves both roles with different surface areas.
Compliance data freshness
Carrier insurance and licence status are assumed to come from an external compliance verification service, not a manually maintained database. Stale data must be surfaced as a warning state, not treated as current.
Data table performance
Tables must handle thousands of active logistical records without UI latency. The design assumes virtualised row rendering at the component level — the UX decisions (compact rows, column-fixed layout) were made with this constraint in mind.
07  /  Design Principles

Constraints that resolved decisions, not values on a wall.

These principles were defined before any UI work began. Their value was practical: when a design decision was contested, the principle was the arbiter. Each one traces directly to a research finding.

01

Density over elegance

Dispatchers scan hundreds of rows under operational pressure. Spacious consumer-style layouts are a liability. Every pixel earns its place or it’s removed.

02

State is never ambiguous

Status indicators pair colour with text. Always. Colour alone fails under stress, fails for colour-blind users, and violates WCAG 2.2 SC 1.4.1. Non-negotiable.

03

Context is never broken

No full-page navigations mid-task. A dispatcher investigating a vehicle should never lose their place in the master list. Side drawers, not detail pages.

04

The system prevents errors

Where a wrong action has downstream consequences — wrong vehicle type, lapsed insurance — the UI blocks it before it happens. Not a warning after. A lock before.

08  /  Information Architecture

Structured around roles, not database tables.

A common failure pattern in enterprise systems is IA designed to reflect the data model rather than the operational workflow. Each module in LogiFlow maps to a dispatcher or manager task sequence — not to a backend entity. The naming was treated as a design decision, not a label.

⬡ LogiFlow ERP — Master Hub
Dashboard
  • Operations overview
  • Risk alert feed
Deals
  • Active deliveries
  • Pending approvals
Fleet
  • Vehicle registry
  • Dispatch map
Carrier Network
  • Carrier database
  • Compliance logs
Analytics
  • Revenue vs costs
  • Geographic heatmap

Naming as design: The initial label “Contractors” mapped to IT procurement in dispatchers’ mental models, not carrier management. Simulated task flows revealed this would cause consistent findability failures for new staff. Renamed to “Carrier Network” to match the vocabulary of logistics operators.

09  /  Key Design Decisions

Decisions as tradeoffs, not features.

Each decision below represents a tradeoff — something gained and something deliberately foregone. Presenting them as features would obscure the reasoning.

3-zone dashboard layout: KPIs, operations queue, live risk alerts

Layout

Research into high-density enterprise screen scanning suggests users develop structured F and Z reading patterns. The dashboard enforces a fixed hierarchy: global health at the top strip, primary operational data in the centre, high-urgency exceptions isolated in the right sidebar. Operations managers can triage systemic state in under 10 seconds.

Tradeoff: The right sidebar reduces the width of the central table on smaller displays. This was accepted — the platform is desktop-first, and removing the risk feed would collapse visibility for the manager persona.

Tabular data over cards for dense operational scanning

Data density

Card grids distribute information across two axes, requiring the eye to reorient per card. For a dispatcher scanning 100+ active shipments, fixed-width columnar alignment allows vertical scanning down a single axis — status, value, vehicle — without saccadic movement. This directly reduces cognitive load under time pressure.

Tradeoff: Tables are less visually modern and carry a higher onboarding cost for new users. Accepted — operational efficiency for experienced dispatchers outweighs aesthetic preference.

Side drawers over detail pages: preserving navigational context

Progressive disclosure

Navigating to a full detail page severs the dispatcher’s context in the master list. A right-aligned side drawer allows investigation of fleet geolocation, compliance records, or deal details while the master list remains visible and filterable. Focus trap, keyboard-accessible, and returns focus to the triggering row on close.

Tradeoff: Drawers reduce visible table width by approximately 50% when open. The V2 fluid reflow response (table compresses left rather than being covered) was designed specifically to address this.

Semantic status: colour and text always paired

Accessibility

Relying on colour alone to communicate operational state violates WCAG 2.2 SC 1.4.1 and creates genuine ambiguity under stress. Every status in the system pairs a colour token with explicit text. This isn’t a compliance add-on — it’s the correct decision regardless of regulation, given the cognitive demands of the environment.

Tradeoff: Text labels reduce the information density achievable in status columns. Accepted without hesitation — ambiguous status in a time-critical workflow is not a defensible tradeoff.
10  /  Accessibility

WCAG 2.2 AA as a design input, not a post-hoc audit.

Logistics dispatch is a high-demand environment: long shifts, variable lighting, time pressure, and high cognitive load. Accessible design — high contrast, predictable navigation, explicit error states, keyboard operability — directly reduces error rates in this context. It was treated as a design constraint from day one, not a compliance layer applied after the fact.

WCAG 2.2 Level AA — Target Conformance Standard

The following documents the specific criteria addressed, the design decisions made to satisfy them, and gaps flagged for future iteration. Where WCAG 2.2 introduced new criteria (marked below), these were treated as first-class design requirements, not additions to an existing audit.

Perceivable

Colour independence for all status states

1.4.1 AA

Every operational status pairs a colour token with explicit text. No information is communicated by colour alone. Protects approximately 8% of the male dispatcher population affected by colour vision deficiency.

ImplementedUse of Colour

Contrast ratios validated against all backgrounds

1.4.3 AA

Status pill text was validated against its specific pill background, not just the page background. The initial “In Transit” blue achieved only 3.2:1 and was revised to 5.1:1 before any evaluation. Dark sidebar against white cards exceeds 7:1.

ImplementedContrast (Minimum)

Non-text contrast — component boundaries

1.4.11 AA

All interactive component boundaries (checkboxes, dropdowns, pagination) achieve at least 3:1 against adjacent colours. Icon-only buttons include visible borders to satisfy this without relying on hover states alone.

ImplementedNon-text Contrast

Text resizing without content loss

1.4.4 AA

Data tables use fluid column widths. At 200% zoom the layout reflows rather than clips. No content hidden behind overflow at standard browser zoom levels.

ImplementedResize Text

Contrast validation: All four status colours pass WCAG 2.2 AA 1.4.3. “Delayed” red (#c84b2f on #fdf0ec) achieves 4.8:1. “In Transit” blue achieves 5.1:1. “Active” green achieves 5.4:1. “Pending” amber achieves 4.9:1. Colour is supplementary — text labels are always present as the primary signal.

● Delayed4.8:1 — AA Pass
● In Transit5.1:1 — AA Pass
● Active5.4:1 — AA Pass
● Pending4.9:1 — AA Pass

Operable

Full keyboard navigation

2.1.1 AA

All interactive elements are reachable and operable via keyboard. Tab order follows logical reading order. Side drawers trap focus while open and return focus to the triggering element on close — preventing the common “focus lost to top of page” failure.

ImplementedKeyboard Accessible

Visible focus indicators

2.4.11 AA

New in WCAG 2.2. Focus indicators meet the minimum area and contrast thresholds. 2px solid ring in brand green (#2d6a47) with white offset, achieving 3:1 on both light and dark surfaces. Focus is never suppressed.

ImplementedFocus Appearance (2.2)

Skip navigation link

2.4.1 AA

A visually hidden “Skip to main content” link is the first focusable element on every page, visible on focus. Critical for keyboard users who would otherwise tab through the full left-rail navigation on every page load.

ImplementedBypass Blocks

Draggable movements — pointer alternative

2.5.7 AA

New in WCAG 2.2. Any future drag interactions (reorder columns, drag-to-assign on the fleet map) must have a single-pointer alternative. Flagged as a V3 design constraint before any drag patterns are introduced.

Monitor V3Dragging Movements (2.2)

Understandable

Error identification and description

3.3.1 AA

Missing cargo fields during document generation are highlighted amber with a text label describing what is missing and what action to take. Colour change alone is not used. Satisfies SC 3.3.3 (Error Suggestion) at AA as a by-product.

ImplementedError Identification

Accessible names for all interactive elements

4.1.2 AA

All icon-only controls include aria-label attributes. Table columns use proper th scope attributes. Status pills include programmatic names for screen readers that do not announce visual colour.

ImplementedName, Role, Value

Consistent navigation across all modules

3.2.3 AA

The left-rail, top search bar, and module header actions appear in identical positions across all five modules. Spatial consistency reduces reorientation cost — particularly important for users with cognitive disabilities who rely on predictable layout.

ImplementedConsistent Navigation

Target size for interactive controls

2.5.8 AA

New in WCAG 2.2. Interactive targets must be at least 24×24 CSS pixels. Ellipsis action menus and pagination arrows in the compact table layout were identified at 20×20px — borderline. Flagged for a V2 fix via padding adjustment without expanding row height.

Flagged for fixTarget Size (2.2)

Poka-Yoke guardrails as accessible error prevention (SC 3.3.4)

The guardrail that disables “Assign Fleet” when cargo type mismatches vehicle capability serves dual purposes. For all users it prevents a high-consequence operational error. For screen reader users who may not have scanned the vehicle type column, it removes the need to cross-reference — the system communicates the constraint through UI state and a tooltip, not through visual scanning.

WCAG 2.2 SC 3.3.4 requires that inputs with legal or financial consequences can be reviewed, confirmed, or reversed. Preventing the error entirely is the highest possible satisfaction of this criterion.

11  /  Heuristic Evaluation

A simulated walkthrough, then an expert review.

The V1 design was evaluated using a formal cognitive walkthrough simulating four mission-critical dispatch tasks, followed by a structured expert review against Nielsen’s 10 usability heuristics. The walkthrough was simulated — not conducted with real users — using the modelled dispatcher persona and the as-is workflow sequences.

Methodology note: This is a simulated expert evaluation, not empirical usability testing. Findings represent predicted failure modes based on established heuristics and workflow modelling, not observed user behaviour. This distinction matters for how findings should be weighted.

Heuristic & finding
Severity
Error prevention — cargo/vehicle mismatch
No guardrail prevented assigning a flatbed to refrigerated cargo. A high-consequence error with no system intervention.
High
Help users recover from errors — GPS API failure
Map silently blanked on GPS feed drop. Users would likely interpret missing trucks as operational, not as a data feed failure.
High
Visibility of system status — async operations
PDF generation and GPS fetch had no loading indicator. Predicted to cause duplicate submissions under time pressure.
Medium
Recognition over recall — drawer occlusion
Fleet map drawer covered 50% of the data table, requiring dispatchers to memorise values to cross-reference deal SLAs.
Medium
Flexibility — no bulk fleet assignment
“Assign Fleet” buried in an overflow menu. No multi-row selection for power users managing multiple concurrent shipments.
Medium
Match between system and real world — module naming
“Contractors” maps to IT procurement in the mental model of logistics staff, not carrier management.
Low
Consistency & standards
No violations. IBM Carbon Design System geometry and padding applied consistently across all modules.
Pass
Aesthetic & minimalist design
No noise violations. Dark mode reduces eye fatigue on extended shifts. Information-to-noise ratio intentionally high throughout.
Pass
12  /  Iterations

Each fix traces to a specific failure. No polish for its own sake.

Heuristic 5 fix

Poka-Yoke cargo/vehicle guardrails

Problem

The fleet assignment interface allowed any vehicle type to be assigned to any cargo type. A flatbed assigned to refrigerated cargo is a high-consequence error — and the V1 design offered no system intervention.

Fix & reasoning

If cargo type ≠ vehicle capability, the “Assign” CTA disables with a tooltip explaining the mismatch. Prevents the error entirely rather than flagging it after confirmation — the highest standard of error prevention per WCAG 3.3.4.

Heuristic 6 fix

Fluid detail architecture — table reflow on drawer open

Problem

The side drawer covered 50% of the data table when open. Dispatchers needing to cross-reference a deal SLA had to close the drawer, read the table, and reopen it — a memorisation burden that grows with time pressure.

Fix & reasoning

Opening the drawer compresses the table via a responsive grid shift rather than overlaying it. Both datasets remain visible simultaneously. This satisfies heuristic 6 (recognition over recall) without sacrificing the drawer pattern.

Heuristic 1 fix

Skeleton loaders and toast confirmations for async operations

Problem

PDF generation and GPS fetch had no loading states. The absence of feedback is predicted to cause duplicate submissions when dispatchers re-click under time pressure, with no visible system response.

Fix & reasoning

Skeleton loaders on tables; explicit toast notifications for async background tasks; CTAs disable on first click and re-enable on completion. The system always communicates its current state.

Research finding fix

Critical alert banner — cross-module risk visibility

Problem

Simulated walkthrough revealed that SLA breaches and critical delays were only visible in the Deals module. A dispatcher working in the Fleet module would have no awareness of a newly escalated delay.

Fix & reasoning

V2 introduces a persistent critical alert banner that sits above all module content. High-severity events surface system-wide, regardless of which module the dispatcher currently occupies.

13  /  Projected Impact

Directional outcomes, not claimed results.

These are projected outcomes based on the workflow analysis and design decisions made — not measured results from deployment. They represent the expected direction of change if the system were implemented against the simulated baseline.

↓

Context-switching overhead

Unifying GPS, deal tracking, and compliance into a single view should significantly reduce the per-shift time currently lost to tool-switching. Workflow simulation suggests 30–45 minutes of potential recovery per shift.

↓

Manual data re-entry

Automatic data inheritance during document generation should eliminate most manual transcription. The CMR workflow would reduce from a multi-step copy-paste process to a single triggered action.

↓

Compliance risk exposure

Embedding carrier validation in the primary dispatch flow, with system-enforced guardrails, should reduce the frequency of compliance steps being skipped under pressure — the primary risk mechanism identified in research.

↑

Management visibility

A live KPI dashboard and automated risk alerts would shift operations managers from reactive information-gathering to proactive oversight — a structural change in how management oversight functions.

Note on metrics: This case study deliberately avoids specific percentage claims or quantified outcomes. These would require real deployment and measurement data that does not exist for a concept project. The value of this work lies in the reasoning behind the design decisions, not in fabricated results.

14  /  Reflection

What the process produced. What the next iteration demands.

What I’d keep

  • Defining design principles before UI work — they functioned as arbitration criteria, not aspirations
  • Treating heuristic evaluation as hypothesis generation, not validation — the distinction matters for how findings are weighted
  • Mapping every V2 change to a specific evaluation finding — removes ambiguity about what is signal versus noise
  • Modelling system constraints explicitly before designing — API dependencies and concurrency shaped architectural decisions early
  • WCAG 2.2 AA as a design input from day one — including the four new 2.2 criteria as first-class requirements

What the next iteration needs

  • Empirical usability testing with actual dispatchers — the simulated walkthrough is a prediction; real sessions would surface failure modes the simulation cannot anticipate
  • Card sorting on the IA before committing to module structure — the naming issue surfaced late in simulation; it would have appeared in week one with real participants
  • Quantitative evaluation metrics — task completion rates and time-on-task to replace directional outcome projections
  • Bulk assignment pattern tested separately — a high-leverage dispatcher workflow that deserved its own evaluation sprint
  • WCAG 2.5.8 target size audit earlier — the compact table created a 24×24px constraint that required late-stage rework
15  /  Prototype

See the system in motion.

The interactive prototype covers all five modules — Dashboard, Deals, Fleet, Carrier Network, and Analytics — with live status states, side drawer interactions, and the V2 critical alert system.

Open interactive prototype
